1: Vaya’an ’Eliphaz ha-Temani (then Eliphaz the Temanite replied) — the standard dialogue introduction. Eliphaz is the first and most theologically sophisticated of the three friends. His speeches (Job 4–5, 15, 22) represent the most developed defense of the retributive justice principle. Teman is associated with wisdom in Jeremiah 49:7 (“Is wisdom no more in Teman?”), making Eliphaz a representative of the best available wisdom tradition outside Israel.
2: Hannissa davar ’elekha tila’eh (if one ventures a word with you, will it trouble you?) — nasa’ = to lift up/carry/attempt; tila’eh = to be weary/troubled. Eliphaz begins with a rhetorical apology for speaking, acknowledging the sensitivity of the moment. Va’atsor be-millim mi yukal (who can hold back from speaking?) — implying the inner compulsion to offer wisdom overrides the caution to remain silent.
3: Hinneh yissarta rabbim (look, you have instructed many people) — yasar in the pi’el = to discipline, instruct, train. Eliphaz acknowledges Job’s prior role as a teacher and strengthener of others. Veyadayim rafot tezaqzeq (and you have strengthened weak hands) — rafot = drooping/hanging loose; hazaq = to strengthen/make firm. Eliphaz is not contemptuous: he concedes Job’s past ministry.
4: Mikhshol yakim millatekha (your words have upheld the one who was stumbling) — mikhshol = the one who stumbles, literally “a stumbling one.” Birkhayim kor’ot te’ammets (you have steadied feeble knees) — kor’ot = bending/buckling knees. The very knees that Job’s wisdom steadied in others are the knees that buckle in his own crisis (v4 and v5 form the before-and-after contrast).
5: Ki ’atah tavo ’elekha vatilla’ (but now it has come upon you, and you are exhausted) — the pivot: ’atah (now). Vatilla’ = you are weary/impatient. Tigga’ ’adekha vattibbahel (it has touched you, and you are terrified) — tibbahel = to be dismayed/panicked (pi’el passive). Eliphaz’s implicit accusation: Job counseled others admirably but cannot take his own medicine.
6: Halo yir’atekha kislat’kha (is not your fear of God your confidence?) — yir’atekha = your fear (of God) — the same root as Job’s description in Job 1:1 (yere’ ’elohim). Eliphaz directly invokes Job’s own acknowledged piety: if Job truly fears God, that should be his source of confidence (kislah = confidence/trust). Tiqvatekha ve-tom derakhekha (your hope and the integrity of your ways) — tom is the same root as tam (Job 1:1, blameless) and tummah (Job 2:3, 9, integrity). Eliphaz is using Job’s own moral vocabulary against him.
7: Zekhor-na mi hu naqqi ’abad (think: who, being innocent, has perished?) — the rhetorical question is the friends’ foundational axiom, stated most starkly here. Naqqi = innocent, clean, without guilt. Eliphaz’s implied answer: no innocent person has ever perished. This is the retributive justice principle (lex talionis applied to providence): suffering = guilt; innocence = protection. The book’s entire argument is designed to refute this principle, since Job is innocent and suffering.
8: Ka’asher ra’iti horesh ’aven (as I have observed, those who plow trouble/iniquity) — harash ’aven (to plow iniquity) and zore’e ’amal (to sow trouble/mischief). The agricultural metaphor: those who plant wickedness harvest wickedness. ’Amal = trouble/mischief (the same root as the “trouble” Job’s mother’s womb failed to hide, Job 3:10).
9: Minishmat ’eloah yo’vedu (by the breath of God they perish) — nishmah = breath/spirit, the animating breath (Genesis 2:7). Umeruah ’appo yikhlah (and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed) — ’ap = nose/nostril/anger. The dual meaning of ’ap (nose and anger) allows: both the literal blast of YHWH’s nose/breath and his wrath destroy the wicked.
10: She’agat ’aryeh ve-qol shahats (the roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion) — the lion imagery of vv10–11 is a traditional wisdom metaphor for the powerful/arrogant who are eventually brought low. Shinnei keppirim nittu’u (the teeth of the young lions are broken/shattered) — the powerful who rule by force will have their instruments of power destroyed.
11: Layish ’oved me-bli-teref (the mature lion perishes for lack of prey) — layish = a mature/aged lion (only four occurrences in OT; the rare word for the most experienced, dangerous lion). Even the most powerful predator perishes when cut off from its food supply. Ugnei leviyah yitparedu (the cubs of the lioness scatter) — without the dominant lion, the family structure dissolves. The point: the wicked will fall, however strong.
12: Ve-’elay davar yegunnav (a word was stolen/came stealthily to me) — gannav from ganav = to steal. The revelation arrived by stealth, like a thief. Vattiqqah ’ozni shemets mimmennu (and my ear received a whisper of it) — shemets = a whisper, a faint rustling sound (only here in the OT). The divine communication arrived as the faintest possible sound, barely a whisper.
13: Bise’ipim meh ezyonot laylah (in disturbing thoughts from visions of the night) — se’ipim = disturbing/troubling thoughts, branching thoughts (from sa’ap = to divide, branch). The hefal tardema (when deep sleep falls on people) — tardemah = deep sleep, the same word used for Adam’s sleep in Genesis 2:21 and Abraham’s in Genesis 15:12, both precursors of divine revelation.
14: Pahad qera’ani vare’adah (fear came upon me and trembling) — pahad = terror/fear (the same word Job used for his own terror in Job 3:25). Vere’adah = trembling. Ve-rov ’atsmotay hiyhid (making all my bones shake) — hiyhid = to make tremble (hiphil). The theophanic experience is described through its physical effects on the recipient.
15: Veruah ’al-panay yahhalof (a spirit passed before my face) — ruah = breath/spirit/wind; the theophanic presence passing before the recipient (cf. Elijah’s cave experience, 1 Kings 19:11–13, where ‘passing before’ is the theophanic pattern). Yisammer se’ar besari (the hair of my flesh bristled) — a precise physical description of the goosebump response to numinous terror.
16: Ya’amod ve-lo ’akir mareihu (it stood still, but I could not discern its form) — the spirit is present but invisible; it stops, but without form (temunah). Temunah = shape/form/likeness (same word as Numbers 12:8: Moses saw the temunah of YHWH; Deuteronomy 4:12: no form seen at Sinai; Psalms 17:15: seeing God’s temunah in the afterlife). The revelation to Eliphaz withholds what Moses received: no form, only a voice.
17: Ha-ye’enosh me-’eloah yitsdak (can a mortal be more righteous than God?) — ’enosh = the frail human, the mortal (as opposed to ’ish, gever, or ’adam). The choice of ’enosh emphasizes human fragility and mortality. Yitsdak = be righteous, be vindicated (in the forensic sense). The rhetorical question expects a negative answer: of course not. But the book’s subsequent argument will complicate this: YHWH himself says Job ‘spoke what is right’ (Job 42:7).
18: Hen ba’avadav lo ya’amin (God places no trust even in his servants) — ’eved = servant; the divine servants (angels) are not trusted by God without question. Uvemal’akhav yasim teholah (he charges his angels with error) — toholah = folly, error (only here and Jeremiah 23:13, where it is used for the prophets’ error). Eliphaz’s argument: if God finds error even in his angelic servants, how much more in mortals made of clay?
19: Af shokhnei batei-homer (how much more those who dwell in houses of clay) — batei-homer = houses of clay. Homer = clay, the stuff from which humans are made (Genesis 2:7, afar min-ha’adamah = dust from the ground; but homer also = clay, used in pottery). ’Asher-be’afar yesadam (whose foundation is in the dust) — the foundation of the clay house is the very dust it’s made of. Yedakke’um lifnei-’ash (who are crushed more easily than a moth) — the comparison: humans are more fragile than a moth (which dies at a touch).
20: Miboqer la’erev yikkattu (between morning and evening they are destroyed) — the full length of a day is more than enough time for human life to end. Mibbeli measim ye’avdu lanetsah (without anyone noticing, they vanish forever) — netsah = forever/permanently. Human death goes largely unnoticed in the cosmic scheme: the world continues without marking individual human exits.
21: Halo nissa’ yiteram bam (is not their tent-peg pulled up from within them?) — a difficult phrase. Yeter = cord/tent-peg/stake. The tent-peg (yeter) that holds the structure upright, removed from within — the human body as a tent whose internal structure collapses at death (2 Corinthians 5:1 uses the same metaphor: “if the earthly tent [house] we live in is destroyed”). Yamuthu velo behokhma (they die, and that without gaining wisdom) — behokhma = in/with wisdom. The final word: human life ends before wisdom is acquired. This is the final argument of Eliphaz’s first stanza, and it sets up what Job 28’s great wisdom hymn will address: wisdom is inaccessible to mortal searching.
